title: Polynomial special products: difference of squares
description: Find products of binomials that have the special form "difference of squares."
topic_path: Polynomial special products: difference of squares

assessment_items.question_content:
** Expand and combine like terms.**

$(4b^2+3)(4b^2-3)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We can expand this expression like any product of two binomials. However, this expression has a special form that makes it easier to expand.

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)(P-Q)=P^2-Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
** Expand and combine like terms.**

$(8-n^7)(8+n^7)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We can expand this expression like any product of two binomials. However, this expression has a special form that makes it easier to expand.

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)(P-Q)=P^2-Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
** Expand and combine like terms.**

$(2x^4+3x^3)(2x^4-3x^3)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We can expand this expression like any product of two binomials. However, this expression has a special form that makes it easier to expand.

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)(P-Q)=P^2-Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(2x^4+3x^3)(2x^4-3x^3)
\\\\
&=\left(2x^4\right)^2-\left(3x^3\right)^2
\\\\
&=4x^8-9x^6
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
** Expand and combine like terms.**

$(5a^3-6a^2)(5a^3+6a^2)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We can expand this expression like any product of two binomials. However, this expression has a special form that makes it easier to expand.

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)(P-Q)=P^2-Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(5a^3-6a^2)(5a^3+6a^2)
\\\\
&=\left(5a^3\right)^2-\left(6a^2\right)^2
\\\\
&=25a^6-36a^4
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
** Expand and combine like terms.**

$(7b^5-4b)(7b^5+4b)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We can expand this expression like any product of two binomials. However, this expression has a special form that makes it easier to expand.

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)(P-Q)=P^2-Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(7b^5-4b)(7b^5+4b)
\\\\
&=\left(7b^5\right)^2-\left(4b\right)^2
\\\\
&=49b^{10}-16b^2
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
** Expand and combine like terms.**

$(9c^2+c^6)(9c^2-c^6)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We can expand this expression like any product of two binomials. However, this expression has a special form that makes it easier to expand.

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)(P-Q)=P^2-Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(9c^2+c^6)(9c^2-c^6)
\\\\
&=\left(9c^2\right)^2-\left(c^6\right)^2
\\\\
&=81c^4-c^{12}
\end{align}$

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
** Expand and combine like terms.**

$(8n^2+3n^7)(8n^2-3n^7)=$ [[☃ expression 1]]
hints:
We can expand this expression like any product of two binomials. However, this expression has a special form that makes it easier to expand.

This is the "difference of squares" form (where $P$ and $Q$ can be any monomial):

$(P+Q)(P-Q)=P^2-Q^2$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}(8n^2+3n^7)(8n^2-3n^7)
\\\\
&=\left(8n^2\right)^2-\left(3n^7\right)^2
\\\\
&=64n^{4}-9n^{14}
\end{align}$
